_comment: Hanuman chant cycle — two canonical Sanskrit verses (the Manojavam dhyāna-śloka and the Hanumat-smaraṇāt-bhavet 'eight blessings' śloka) framed by traditional Hindi devotional invocations (Jai Shri Ram / Jai Hanuman / Jai Bajrang Bali). The 8 lines below cover ONE complete cycle (0:00–3:10). The user reports that the same cycle loops again from 3:10 to the end of the video; that second cycle will not highlight in the lyric panel (a known limitation — useTranslation matches a single linear time range). Timestamps were supplied by the curator from the audio.
title: Hanuman Chant — Manojavam Mantra + Hanumat Smaraṇāt + Jai Shri Ram

lines.explanation: Opening invocation. 'Jai' (जय) is the colloquial Hindi/Bhojpuri pronunciation of Sanskrit 'jaya' (victory); in popular devotional usage the chant 'Jai Shri Ram' is a salutation rather than a metaphysical statement — affirming Rama's victory over forces of disorder. The most common Hindu devotional refrain.

lines.english_literal: Victory to the strong one with thunderbolt-limbs!
lines.english_poetic: Victory to Bajrang Bali — the one with limbs as strong as lightning.
lines.explanation: Invocation to Hanuman. 'Bajrang' is the popular Hindi spelling of Sanskrit 'vajrāṅga' (वज्राङ्ग — vajra 'thunderbolt' + aṅga 'limb'), referring to Hanuman's near-indestructible body. 'Balī' (बली) = strong one. Bajrang Bali is one of the most beloved names for Hanuman in North Indian devotion.

lines.devanagari: मनोजवं मारुततुल्यवेगं जितेन्द्रियं बुद्धिमतां वरिष्ठम् । वातात्मजं वानरयूथमुख्यं श्रीरामदूतं शरणं प्रपद्ये ॥
lines.iast: manojavaṃ māruta-tulya-vegaṃ jitendriyaṃ buddhimatāṃ variṣṭham | vātātmajaṃ vānara-yūtha-mukhyaṃ śrī-rāma-dūtaṃ śaraṇaṃ prapadye ||
lines.english_literal: Mind-swift, of speed equal to the wind, with senses conquered, best among the intelligent; son of the wind, chief of the monkey-troop, messenger of Shri Rama — I take refuge.
lines.english_poetic: I take refuge in him whose speed is the speed of the mind, whose force equals the wind — who has conquered his senses, who is foremost among the wise; the son of the Wind-god, leader of the monkey host, the messenger of Shri Rama.
lines.explanation: The traditional dhyāna-śloka of Hanuman, recited at the opening of Hanuman pūjā and at the start of the Sundara Kāṇḍa of the Rāmacaritamānasa. The verse names eight epithets in a single breath: mano-java (mind-swift), māruta-tulya-vega (wind-paced), jitendriya (sense-conqueror), buddhimatāṃ variṣṭha (foremost-of-the-wise), vātātmaja (wind's-son), vānara-yūtha-mukhya (monkey-troop-leader), śrī-rāma-dūta (Rama's messenger). The mantra is a complete portrait of Hanuman's qualities — physical, intellectual, and devotional. 'Śaraṇaṃ prapadye' (I take refuge) is the standard Vaiṣṇava act of self-surrender.

words.meaning: the wind (also a name for Vāyu)
one whose senses are conquered (jita + indriya)

lines.devanagari: बुद्धिर्बलं यशो धैर्यं निर्भयत्वम् अरोगता । अजाड्यं वाक्पटुत्वं च हनूमत्स्मरणाद् भवेत् ॥
lines.iast: buddhir balaṃ yaśo dhairyaṃ nirbhayatvam arogatā | ajāḍyaṃ vāk-paṭutvaṃ ca hanūmat-smaraṇād bhavet ||
lines.english_literal: Intelligence, strength, fame, firmness, fearlessness, freedom from disease, freedom from sluggishness, and eloquence — (these) come about from remembering Hanuman.
lines.english_poetic: Intelligence, strength, fame, and courage; fearlessness and freedom from illness; an alert mind and eloquence in speech — all these arise from the remembrance of Hanuman.
lines.explanation: The famous 'Hanumat-smaraṇāt-bhavet' śloka — one of the most widely quoted Hanuman verses, often inscribed on Hanuman temple walls. It enumerates **eight blessings** that come from merely remembering Hanuman: (1) buddhi (intellect), (2) bala (physical strength), (3) yaśas (fame), (4) dhairya (courage), (5) nirbhayatva (fearlessness), (6) arogatā (freedom from disease), (7) ajāḍya (freedom from sluggishness / mental dullness), (8) vāk-paṭutva (skill in speech). The optative 'bhavet' (one would obtain) keeps the verse as a promise rather than a guarantee — the blessings arise as a natural consequence of the remembering.

lines.devanagari: मनोजवं मारुततुल्यवेगं जितेन्द्रियं बुद्धिमतां वरिष्ठम् । वातात्मजं वानरयूथमुख्यं श्रीरामदूतं शरणं प्रपद्ये ॥
lines.iast: manojavaṃ māruta-tulya-vegaṃ jitendriyaṃ buddhimatāṃ variṣṭham | vātātmajaṃ vānara-yūtha-mukhyaṃ śrī-rāma-dūtaṃ śaraṇaṃ prapadye ||
lines.english_poetic: (The Manojavam mantra returns, sealing the prayer-sequence.)
lines.explanation: Reprise of the Manojavam dhyāna-śloka, sung after the smaraṇāt verse to close the prayer-sequence. The full word-by-word breakdown is on the earlier occurrence of this verse (line 2 above).
Closing invocation — the cycle returns to where it began.

Direct invocation to Hanuman by his Sanskrit name. 'Hanumān' (हनुमान्) is conventionally derived from 'hanu' (jaw) + the possessive '-mat' suffix, referring to his distinctive jaw — a feature in the well-known childhood story of Hanuman trying to swallow the sun and being struck by Indra's vajra.

words.meaning: Hanuman (the one with the distinctive jaw)

lines.english_poetic: Victory to Bajrang Bali — the thunderbolt-strong one!
lines.explanation: Final repetition of the Bajrang-Bali invocation, closing the cycle. The audio continues to loop the same content (Jai Shri Ram → Jai Bajrang Bali → Manojavam → smaraṇāt → Jai Shri Ram → Jai Hanuman → Jai Bajrang Bali) for the remainder of the video.
